title: The Intelligence Age
executive_summary: Altman argues we are entering an Intelligence Age as significant as the Agricultural or Industrial Revolutions — where AI will enable individuals and societies to accomplish things that would have seemed like magic to earlier generations
The essay is optimistic in the extreme: Altman believes AI will generate so much shared prosperity that the future cannot yet be written about with any justice
The core driver is the scaling of deep learning — which Altman argues worked, got predictably better with compute, and will continue to do so
Altman frames the transition as one that requires active engagement and investment, not caution or abstention — institutions that stand back will not shape how the gains are distributed

For wealth management, the essay's central implication is the one Altman does not spell out: if the cost of intelligence approaches zero, every service built on access to expertise faces a structural repricing
the_one_insight: If deep learning continues to scale as it has — and Altman believes it will — the cost of intelligence collapses. Every business model built on information asymmetry is repriced. Wealth management is near the top of that list.

key_quotes.text: In the next couple of decades, we will be able to do things that would have seemed like magic to our grandparents.
key_quotes.context: On the scale of transformation ahead

key_quotes.text: I believe the future is going to be so bright that no one can do it justice by trying to write about it now.
key_quotes.context: On the limits of forecasting the Intelligence Age

key_quotes.text: Deep learning worked, got predictably better with scale, and we dedicated increasing resources to it.
key_quotes.context: On why the current AI moment was inevitable, not surprising
